medications.drug_class: topical imidazole antifungal with a mild (low-potency) topical corticosteroid
medications.indication_rationale: A topical antifungal clears the candidal overgrowth and the added mild corticosteroid rapidly settles the intense burning and inflammation — the source note's prescribed treatment. In telehealth this is obtainable via a pharmacist or the reviewing GP; apply only after the folds have been gently dried, and only a low-potency steroid combination (a potent steroid alone would worsen a fungal infection).
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the affected skin folds twice daily (dose per source note; do not exceed) until clear, typically 1-2 weeks
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not use a potent topical corticosteroid alone on this fungal eruption
Stop and seek review if signs of secondary bacterial infection develop (spreading redness, heat, thick pus, fever)

medications.indication_rationale: Applied over the treated folds to reduce friction and protect the macerated skin from moisture, speeding healing and easing the friction pain — per the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a protective layer over the affected folds after the antifungal cream has been applied, and after drying (per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: An unopposed potent topical steroid worsens and masks a candidal infection (tinea/candida incognito) and thins already-macerated fold skin — a dangerous error of commission. Only a low-potency steroid combined with an antifungal is appropriate for the acute inflammation.

No fever, no spreading induration and no purulence — there is no secondary bacterial infection to treat, and broad-spectrum antibiotics precipitated this candidal flare. Prescribing them for serous exudate is over-treatment; reserve strictly for documented secondary cellulitis.

A localised cutaneous candidal intertrigo responds to topical therapy; systemic antifungals are reserved for extensive, recurrent, refractory or mucosal/disseminated disease, none of which is present. Avoids unnecessary drug exposure and interactions.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the skin folds scrupulously dry: gently pat (not rub) fully dry after washing, use a cool hair-dryer or a soft dry cloth/absorbent material in the deep creases, wear loose breathable clothing, and reduce occlusion and sweating.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Candida needs a warm moist occluded surface; eliminating fold moisture is as important as the cream and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Work with the GP on glycaemic control (and, over time, weight) to reduce the deep abdominal/groin folds and the hyperglycaemia that feed the infection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Poorly-controlled diabetes and deep skin folds are the underlying drivers; addressing them prevents recurrence and aids healing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue metformin as prescribed and attend the GP for a diabetes review; do not use leftover or broad-spectrum antibiotics for the skin.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Optimising diabetes control is part of definitive treatment; further antibiotics would worsen the candidal overgrowth.

patient_education_points.point: This is a yeast (fungal) overgrowth in the warm moist skin folds. The recent antibiotics and higher blood-sugar levels let it flourish — it is common and treatable, not an emergency.

The two most important things right now are keeping the folds dry and using an antifungal cream (with a mild anti-inflammatory to calm the burning) twice a day. A barrier paste protects the raw skin from rubbing.

Because you have diabetes and the skin is broken, you should be seen in person soon (within a day or two) so a clinician can look at the folds, check there's no early infection, and review your diabetes.

Get urgent help if the redness spreads well beyond the folds, the skin becomes hot and hard, the fluid turns into thick or foul-smelling pus, or you develop a fever or the shivers — these suggest a bacterial infection taking hold.
Getting your blood sugars under better control will speed healing and stop this coming back.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt in-person GP review within 24-72 hours to inspect the folds and check diabetic control, then again if not clearly improving within a further 3-5 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the intertrigo to topical treatment, skin integrity in the folds, any early secondary bacterial infection, and glycaemic control
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness beyond the folds, heat/hardness of the skin, thick or foul-smelling pus, fever or rigors, or severe worsening pain — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise a common candidal intertrigo of the skin folds (not an emergency) from the classic history/video pattern
Instruct patient-performable measures: keep the folds dry (gentle patting, cool-air drying, loose clothing) and protect the skin
Advise a topical antifungal (with, at most, a low-potency anti-inflammatory) rather than a potent steroid alone or oral antibiotics
Actively screen for, and safety-net against, secondary bacterial cellulitis / sepsis with explicit T4/T5 triggers
Arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within ~24-72 hours) given diabetes and broken skin, and address glycaemic control
acceptable_alternatives: A topical antifungal alone (e.g. clotrimazole/miconazole) with the anti-inflammatory deferred, plus a barrier, is acceptable
Deferring the exact prescription to the reviewing GP/pharmacist rather than specifying a combination product sight-unseen is acceptable
Same-day (T3) in-person review is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition given the diabetes and is not scored as an error
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise keeping the folds dry / the drying measures
Failing to screen for or safety-net secondary bacterial cellulitis and systemic sepsis
Failing to arrange any in-person review in a poorly-controlled diabetic with broken skin (pure self-care only)
Failing to address glycaemic control as a driver
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Recommending a potent topical corticosteroid alone on a fungal eruption
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics with no signs of secondary infection
Prescribing a systemic antifungal for a localised cutaneous intertrigo
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the candidal intertrigo and advising drying + a topical antifungal, (b) an explicit secondary-infection safety-net with T4/T5 triggers, and (c) arranging prompt in-person review with attention to diabetes. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, or a potent-steroid-only / empiric-antibiotic answer, is a scored error regardless of other content; so is giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net at all.
